narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, an LSAT score of 41, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 75% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a risk level that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment comparing your profile to others who have taken the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled to the model a dip in later academic performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 5, slightly below the average of 5.71, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic standing relative to peers. On a positive note, your LSAT score of 41—stronger than the average of 36.70—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and showing your aptitude in that area. While your solid UGPA, family income, and full-time status (common among 93% of students) played roles, they weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile stands out with that higher LSAT but is held back by the lower deciles, which the model sees as stronger bar exam predictors in past data. This doesn't define your abilities, especially as a determined Hispanic student navigating these paths. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT-level prep to amplify your strengths, and reapplying after building a stronger academic trajectory. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your next application could tell a different story.
